title: English Advanced
desc: Master advanced English skills: reading, writing, and critical thinking in English.вЃ вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЃ

lessons.title: Reading Comprehension Strategies
lessons.content:
Active Reading: Beyond Just Words

Reading isn't just moving your eyes across words вЂ” it's an active conversation with the text. Good readers constantly predict, question, connect, and evaluate what they read.

The SQ3R Method

Survey вЂ” Skim headings, images, and bold text first.
Question вЂ” Turn headings into questions ("The Industrial Revolution" в†’ "What was the Industrial Revolution?")
Read вЂ” Read actively, looking for answers to your questions.
Recite вЂ” After each section, summarize in your own words.
Review вЂ” After finishing, review your notes and key ideas.

рџ“– Studies show that active readers retain 50-70% more information than passive readers. The difference isn't intelligence вЂ” it's strategy.
рџ“љ Try this: Before reading a Wikipedia article, read ONLY the section headings. Then ask yourself what you expect to learn. Now read the full article. You'll understand it much faster because your brain already has a "map."

Context Clues

Don't know a word? Don't reach for the dictionary immediately. Look at the context:

вЂў What comes before and after the word?
вЂў Does the sentence give examples?
вЂў Are there contrast words (but, however, unlike)?

"A reader lives a thousand lives before he dies. The man who never reads lives only one." вЂ” George R.R. Martin

quiz.q: What is the best strategy for understanding a difficult text?
o: Preview headings, read actively, and summarize key points
quiz.exp: Active reading вЂ” previewing, highlighting, and summarizing вЂ” helps you understand and retain complex texts.

lessons.title: Writing Paragraphs
lessons.content:
The Building Block of Writing

A paragraph is the fundamental unit of writing. Master paragraphs, and you can write anything вЂ” essays, reports, stories, emails.

The Paragraph Structure

1. Topic Sentence вЂ” States the main idea. Everything else in the paragraph supports it.
2. Supporting Sentences вЂ” Evidence, examples, explanations that prove the topic sentence.
3. Concluding Sentence вЂ” Wraps up the paragraph, transitions to the next.

вњЌпёЏ Topic: "Exercise has significant mental health benefits." Support: "Studies show 30 minutes of exercise reduces anxiety by 20%. Endorphins released during physical activity improve mood naturally." Conclusion: "For these reasons, daily exercise is one of the simplest tools for emotional well-being."
рџ“ќ The "so what?" test: After writing your topic sentence, ask "so what?" If you can't answer with interesting supporting evidence, your topic needs work.

Common Mistakes

вЂў No topic sentence вЂ” The reader doesn't know what the paragraph is about
вЂў Multiple topics вЂ” One paragraph = one idea
вЂў No evidence вЂ” Opinions without support aren't persuasive

"Easy reading is hard writing." вЂ” Nathaniel Hawthorne

o: The last sentence of a paragraph
A sentence that introduces the main idea of a paragraph
quiz.exp: A topic sentence introduces the main idea and guides the reader about what to expect.

lessons.title: Phrasal Verbs That Matter
lessons.content:
Why Phrasal Verbs Matter

Phrasal verbs are combinations of verbs + prepositions/adverbs that create completely new meanings. They're everywhere in English вЂ” and they're what separates textbook English from real English.

Essential Phrasal Verbs

Give up = stop trying. "Never give up on your dreams."
Figure out = solve/understand. "I need to figure out this math problem."
Look forward to = anticipate with pleasure. "I'm looking forward to the vacation."
Come up with = create/invent. "She came up with a brilliant idea."
Put off = postpone. "Don't put off studying until the night before."
Turn out = result. "The project turned out better than expected."
Break down = stop working / analyze. "My car broke down." / "Let's break down this problem."

рџ’Ў Phrasal verbs are informal вЂ” perfect for conversation and casual writing. In formal writing, use the Latin equivalent: "give up" в†’ "abandon"; "figure out" в†’ "determine"; "put off" в†’ "postpone".
рџ—ЈпёЏ "I was looking into the issue when I came across some interesting data. It turns out the problem was easier to sort out than we thought. Let's go over the results together." вЂ” This is how native speakers actually talk!
"Learning a language is not about memorizing rules вЂ” it's about absorbing patterns." вЂ” Stephen Krashen

quiz.exp: "Give up" means to stop trying or quit.

lessons.title: Conditionals and Hypotheticals
lessons.content:
The Power of "If"

Conditionals let you talk about possibilities, hypotheticals, and imaginary situations. English has four main types:

Zero Conditional (Universal Truths)

If + present в†’ present. "If you heat water to 100В°C, it boils."

First Conditional (Real Possibility)

If + present в†’ will + verb. "If it rains tomorrow, I will stay home."

Second Conditional (Hypothetical/Unlikely)

If + past simple в†’ would + verb. "If I won the lottery, I would travel the world."

Third Conditional (Impossible Past)

If + had + past participle в†’ would have + past participle. "If I had studied harder, I would have passed the exam."

рџЋЇ Key insight: the second conditional uses PAST tense but refers to PRESENT/FUTURE. "If I were rich" doesn't mean you were rich before вЂ” it means "imagine being rich right now."
рџ’­ Second conditional is perfect for dreams and advice:
"If I were you, I would save more money."
"What would you do if you could fly?"
"If we had more time, we could finish the project."
"The limits of my language mean the limits of my world." вЂ” Ludwig Wittgenstein

quiz.q: Which sentence uses the second conditional correctly?
o: If I study, I will pass.
If I studied harder, I would pass.
If I had studied, I would have passed.
I study if I have time.
quiz.exp: Second conditional (If + past simple, would + verb) is used for hypothetical present/future situations.

lessons.title: Passive Voice in Action
lessons.sub: When the action matters more than the actor
lessons.content:
Active vs Passive

Active: "The chef prepared the meal." (focus on chef)
Passive: "The meal was prepared by the chef." (focus on meal)

Passive voice puts the receiver of the action in the subject position. Use it when WHO did it is less important than WHAT happened.

When to Use Passive

вЂў Scientific writing: "The experiment was conducted..." (not "I conducted...")
вЂў News: "Three people were injured..." (the victims matter more)
вЂў Unknown agent: "My bike was stolen." (you don't know who)
вЂў Formal/diplomatic: "Mistakes were made." (avoiding blame)

вљ пёЏ Don't overuse passive voice! Active voice is usually clearer and stronger. "The company fired 200 employees" is better than "200 employees were fired by the company" вЂ” unless you want to emphasize the employees.
рџ“° News headline comparison:
Active: "Police arrest suspect" вЂ” focuses on police action
Passive: "Suspect arrested" вЂ” focuses on the event
Both are valid; choice depends on what matters most.
"The difference between the right word and the almost right word is the difference between lightning and a lightning bug." вЂ” Mark Twain

quiz.q: Which sentence is in the passive voice?
o: The cake was baked by my mother.
She is running in the park.
quiz.exp: In passive voice, the subject receives the action: the cake (subject) was baked (action received).

lessons.title: Debate and Opinion Writing
lessons.content:
Structured Arguments

Writing your opinion isn't just saying "I think X." Strong opinion writing has structure, evidence, and engagement with opposing views.

The PEEL Structure

Point вЂ” State your argument clearly
Evidence вЂ” Give facts, data, examples
Explanation вЂ” Explain why the evidence supports your point
Link вЂ” Connect back to your main argument

вњЌпёЏ Point: "Social media should not be used by children under 13." Evidence: "A 2023 study showed that children who use social media before age 13 have 25% higher rates of anxiety." Explanation: "Developing brains are more vulnerable to social comparison and cyberbullying." Link: "Therefore, age restrictions protect children's mental health."

Useful Phrases

Introducing opinion: "I strongly believe that..." / "In my view..."
Adding evidence: "Research shows that..." / "For example..."
Counterargument: "However, some people argue that..." / "Critics claim..."
Rebuttal: "Nevertheless..." / "While this may be true, it fails to consider..."

рџ’Є The strongest arguments acknowledge opposing views and explain why they're wrong. This is called a "counterargument + rebuttal" вЂ” and it makes your position MUCH more convincing.
"The aim of argument should not be victory, but progress." вЂ” Karl Popper

quiz.q: Which phrase is best for introducing a counterargument?
quiz.exp: Acknowledging opposing views makes your argument stronger and more balanced.

lessons.title: Idioms and Cultural Expressions
lessons.content:
What Are Idioms?

Idioms are expressions where the meaning is completely different from the literal words. "Break the ice" has nothing to do with ice вЂ” it means to start a conversation in an awkward situation.

Essential Idioms

Break the ice вЂ” Start conversation in awkward situation
Hit the nail on the head вЂ” Be exactly right
Piece of cake вЂ” Very easy
Cost an arm and a leg вЂ” Very expensive
Under the weather вЂ” Feeling sick
Once in a blue moon вЂ” Very rarely
The ball is in your court вЂ” It's your turn to decide
Bite off more than you can chew вЂ” Take on too much

рџ—ЈпёЏ Real conversation: "How was the test?" вЂ” "Piece of cake! I hit the nail on the head on every question." вЂ” "Lucky you! I bit off more than I could chew by not studying."
рџЊЌ Warning: idioms are culture-specific! "Break a leg" (good luck in English) makes no sense in Portuguese. And Portuguese "dar com os burros n'ГЎgua" (fail) makes no sense in English.
"Language is the road map of a culture." вЂ” Rita Mae Brown

o: To start a conversation in an awkward situation
quiz.exp: "Break the ice" means to do something to relieve tension or start conversation.

lessons.title: Business English Basics
lessons.content:
Professional English

Business English isn't a different language вЂ” it's the same English with more formality, precision, and diplomacy. Mastering it opens doors internationally.

Email Structure

Greeting: "Dear Mr./Ms. [Last name]," or "Dear [First name]," if you know them
Purpose: State why you're writing in the first sentence
Body: Keep it short, one topic per email
Action: What you need from them
Closing: "Best regards," / "Kind regards," / "Sincerely,"

рџ“§ "Dear Ms. Johnson,

I am writing to follow up on our meeting regarding the Q2 marketing plan. Could you please send me the updated budget by Friday?

Thank you for your time.
Best regards, [Name]"
рџ’ј Formal vs Informal:
"I want to know..." в†’ "I would like to inquire..."
"Can you...?" в†’ "Would you be able to...?"
"Thanks" в†’ "Thank you for your consideration"
"ASAP" в†’ "at your earliest convenience"
"Communication works for those who work at it." вЂ” John Powell

quiz.q: Which is the most appropriate way to start a formal email?
quiz.exp: "Dear Mr./Ms. [Last Name]," is the standard formal greeting in business emails.

lessons.title: Critical Reading: News in English
lessons.content:
Don't Just Read вЂ” Analyze

In the age of information overload, the ability to critically evaluate news in English is essential. Not everything published is true, balanced, or well-researched.

The CRAAP Test

Currency вЂ” When was it published? Is it up to date?
Relevance вЂ” Does it relate to your topic?
Authority вЂ” Who wrote it? What are their credentials?
Accuracy вЂ” Is it supported by evidence? Can you verify claims?
Purpose вЂ” Why was it written? To inform, persuade, sell, entertain?

рџ”Ќ Red flags in news articles:
вЂў No author name or date
вЂў Emotional language instead of facts
вЂў No sources or citations
вЂў The URL looks suspicious
вЂў Only one perspective presented
рџ“° Compare these headlines about the SAME event:
Fox News: "Economy adds 200,000 jobs in strong month"
CNN: "Job growth slows as economy adds only 200,000"
Same fact, opposite framing. Critical reading means recognizing the spin.
"If you don't read the newspaper, you are uninformed. If you do read the newspaper, you are misinformed." вЂ” Mark Twain

quiz.q: What should you check first when evaluating a news article?
o: The source, author credentials, and supporting evidence
quiz.exp: Evaluating source credibility, author expertise, and evidence quality helps distinguish reliable journalism.

lessons.title: Creative Writing Workshop
lessons.content:
Everyone Has a Story

Creative writing isn't just for "talented" people вЂ” it's a skill that improves with practice. And the ability to write well is valuable everywhere: applications, presentations, social media, and personal expression.

Show, Don't Tell

The golden rule of creative writing. Don't tell the reader what to feel вЂ” show them with details.

Telling: "She was sad."
Showing: "She pressed her forehead against the cold window, watching the rain trace crooked paths down the glass, each drop a tiny goodbye."

рџЋЁ Use the five senses: What does your character SEE, HEAR, SMELL, TOUCH, TASTE? Sensory details make writing come alive.
вњЌпёЏ Exercise: Rewrite "The room was scary" using sensory details.

Try: "The floorboards groaned under my weight. A musty smell вЂ” like wet newspapers left in a closet for decades вЂ” hung in the air. Something skittered behind the wall. The single lightbulb flickered twice, then died."

Writing Tips

1. Write first, edit later. Don't censor yourself during the creative phase.
2. Read your work aloud. Your ear catches what your eye misses.
3. Cut unnecessary words. "He walked quickly" в†’ "He hurried." Less is more.
4. Start in the middle of the action. Don't warm up вЂ” jump in.

"You can always edit a bad page. You can't edit a blank page." вЂ” Jodi Picoult

o: Using sensory details (sight, sound, smell, touch, taste)
quiz.exp: Sensory details make writing vivid: instead of "nice garden," write "roses filled the warm air with sweet perfume."
